Name: Broadsheet
Summary: Article publishing pipeline — steganography content engine, article-from-linear skill, and editorial production.
Description:
## Broadsheet

The publishing arm of Tidelands. Everything that turns internal customer needs into public articles, LinkedIn posts, and portfolio content.

### What lives here

* **Steganography Content Engine** — 7 series mapped to an intent taxonomy (hider/finder relationships). Anchor series: "I Hid $1."
* **article-from-linear pipeline** — skill, readiness rubric, PR check, blind validation. The machine that selects and scaffolds the next publishable article from Linear customer requests.
* **LinkedIn editorial cadence** — Friday–Monday sprint, two-lane framework (always-on DMs, batched editorial posts).

### What doesn't live here

* Empire State (HITL agent supervision — "Tinder for agent plans")
* Security Research (findings, bounty work, CVD workflow)
* Infrastructure projects (smallweb, Dagger, Terraform)

### Relationship to other projects

Broadsheet *consumes* customer needs from all other projects. A security finding becomes an article. An infrastructure pattern becomes a post. The publishing pipeline is downstream of everything else.

Name: Empire State
Summary: Swipe-to-approve HITL interface for agentic systems — speculative execution meets dating app UX
Description:
## Empire State

**Swipe-based human-in-the-loop decision surface for autonomous agents.**

Empire State is an MCP-native interface where agents submit execution plans as "dating profiles" and humans approve or reject them via swipe. It sits in front of any agentic orchestration layer (Argo Workflows, Goose, any MCP-speaking agent) and turns the HITL bottleneck into a fast, fluid, preference-learning interaction.

### Core Thesis

The approval queue is a solved problem. What hasn't been built is a **stateful, preference-learning, speculatively-branched** decision interface for agents. Every swipe updates a preference model. The queue reranks. The agent's next proposal is conditioned on accumulated taste. The system learns what kind of plans you approve — and pre-builds branches for both outcomes.

### Key Mechanics

* **Speculative execution**: Agents pre-compute decision trees. A "not" prunes a branch and surfaces the next fork instantly — no blocking on regeneration
* **Buffer on many nots**: A streak of rejections is a branch prediction failure. System pauses swiping and switches to richer input to recalibrate
* **Preference Elo**: Agents and plan types develop trust scores. High-performing agents surface first. Scores decay to prevent stale confidence
* **Card-as-profile**: Each proposal rendered as a dating-style profile — hero image (styleframe/diagram), one-line pitch, confidence score, cost estimate, similarity to past approvals
* **Expressive avatars**: Agent faces express uncertainty, confidence, concern — emotional compression of risk quantification
* **Rate limiting**: Capped approvals per cycle. Scarce signals > noisy unlimited ones
* **Super like**: Long-press for "more like this" — stronger training signal
* **Expiry**: Unreviewed cards auto-prune. Staleness is implicit "not"
* **Undo**: Correct a swipe before side effects execute

### Architecture

* **MCP server** exposing `submit_proposal`, `get_decisions`, `get_preference_model`, `submit_branch_set`
* **Embeddable web UI** (Excalidraw model — hosted app, renders inline, mobile-native)
* **Backend adapter layer**: Argo Workflows (suspend nodes to cards), Goose, any MCP client
* **Pkl constraint layer**: Cards only surface after schema validation — human is last filter, not first
* **Existing infra**: Cronjob grid (0600-0900 x 3 TZ), agents build Argo DAGs, suspend nodes become cards

### Relationship to Other Projects

* **Cyber Farm**: Empire State is the decision surface for security research triage
* **Falada**: Styleframe generation pipeline produces the "profile photos" for cards
* **GAP**: Pkl defines card schemas per domain
* **Goose/Block ecosystem**: Open-source MCP server any Goose user can plug in

### Strategic Position

Every agent framework competes on capability. Nobody competes on the human interaction surface. Empire State stakes out the position that the decision interface for agents isn't conversation — it's curation.
Creator: Robin Jordan

Name: Six Domains, One Stack
Summary: The Grammar as Protocol thesis applied across six domains — SAT math, German grammar, music theory, security scanning, operational scheduling, and argumentation — showing the same four-layer architecture in each.
Creator: Robin Jordan
Latest Update:
🟢 On track | Robin Jordan posted an update on Apr 3

Grammars are compiled expertise. An expensive model writes the rules once. A cheap model enforces them forever.

Two articles lay out the Grammar as Protocol thesis and then prove it across six domains — from SAT math to German grammar to security scanning to argumentation — using the same four-layer architecture in each. The claim is structural, not metaphorical. The same Pkl module, Tree-sitter parser, and XState machine pattern applies everywhere.

Name: Grammar as Protocol
Summary: Grammars as universal protocol for agent distillation — Pkl → Tree-sitter → XState → Argo
Description:
## Thesis

Grammars are compiled expertise. An advanced agent (Opus) authors formal rules (Pkl modules, tree-sitter grammars, XState machines). Lite agents (Haiku) consume them at runtime. The grammar constrains the agent's action space — no parse match, no action. This is the core architecture for Cyber Farm's offensive security platform, proven first through educational applications.

## Architecture Stack

* **Pkl** (design time) — author rules, validate test cases, prove concepts
* **Tree-sitter** (runtime) — parse raw input into typed ASTs
* **XState** (runtime) — model expected state machines, detect violations
* **Argo Workflows** (infrastructure) — container orchestration, fan-out
* **Slack/Linear** (meta-layer) — audit trail, human-in-the-loop

## Domains

1. **SAT Math** — proof of concept, personal learning goal
2. **Music Theory** — Max for Live plugin, on-device grammar + API enrichment
3. **Security (OWASP)** — Cyber Farm product, grammar-constrained offensive agents
4. **Agent System Prompts** — future: parseable rules for AI agent behavior

## Key Insight

The grammar simultaneously defines "what to look for" and "what you're allowed to do." Containment and detection are the same artifact. This makes the system insurable, auditable, and legally defensible.
Creator: Robin Jordan

Name: BountyBench Dagger Calibration Harness
Summary: v0 of PLAN-259: one Lunary bounty, end-to-end, via an explicitly-layered Dagger module tree — published as the Post 1 calibration methodology piece.
Description:
'> **v0 of PLAN-259.** Make one BountyBench target flag-capturable end-to-end through an explicitly-layered Dagger module tree, so the calibration signal stops conflating environment reconstruction with security reasoning.

## Thesis

Per PLAN-259: use Dagger as a disciplined extractor of tacit setup knowledge in BountyBench/BountyTasks. Model concrete **bounty instances as the atomic unit**, factor repeated logic upward into system and environment-pattern modules, and use OWASP-family modules only as reusable capability overlays and reporting abstractions.

The current benchmark conflates three things:

1. hidden operational knowledge
2. environment reconstruction ability
3. actual security reasoning

Whitebox setups collapse (2) and (3) together. This harness isolates (3) by codifying (1) and (2) as explicit, auditable Dagger contracts. The "delta between failure in a messy environment and success after codifying missing infra state" *is* the calibration signal.

## Prior art — why we are not starting from zero

PLAN-328 documents prior art in `null-hype/null-hype2`: a family of Argo Workflows that stand up vulnerable targets from `ttl.sh/*-vulnerable-app` images and run Playwright/agent pods against them. Files observed:

* `fastapi_calibration_workflow.yaml`
* `mlflow_calibration_workflow.yaml`
* `librechat_calibration_workflow.yaml`
* `lunary_advanced_workflow.yaml` — directly clones `https://github.com/bountybench/bountytasks.git` and runs the Lunary bounty exploit after patching the target URL
* `lunary_pod_workflow.yaml`
* `final_calibration_workflow.yaml`
* `debug_app_workflow.yaml`

This prior art **proves the deploy-then-exploit pattern is viable** and gives us one fully-wired BountyTasks integration to port from. What it does *not* do — and what this project fixes:

* environment setup is not codified as explicit Dagger modules with reusable contracts
* hidden setup assumptions are implicit in manifest sidecars, runtime shell, and prebuilt images
* exploit logic and environment reconstruction are tightly coupled
* target image build/provenance is opaque from the repo ([ttl.sh](<http://ttl.sh>) images are consumed, not built in-tree)
* execution unit is ad hoc workflow manifests, not bounty-level reproducible modules

## Scope (v0)

One concrete vertical slice, end-to-end:

* **Target:** Lunary. Chosen because PLAN-328 already demonstrates a working `bountybench/bountytasks` integration — cleanest before/after for the Argo → Dagger port.
* **Layering:** foundation → system → bounty modules only. Capability (OWASP) and task-mode (Detect/Exploit/Patch) modules are explicitly *not* in scope for v0 and will come after the first flag is green.
* **Signal:** flag-capture matcher via the existing BountyTasks exploit script, wrapped in a Dagger function. `expect(output).toContain(flag)` — class-agnostic.
* **Delivery:** one publishable write-up as the Post 1 calibration methodology piece.

## Guardrails (from PLAN-259)

* Only codify **environment** assumptions. Never vuln-specific hints.
* Target source snapshot is immutable. The environment layer is what changes.
* Smoke tests verify "app is in intended runnable state", not "exploit path works".
* Iterative agent fixes must not become benchmark shortcuts — enforced by a diff guardrail Dagger function that rejects PRs touching the frozen target snapshot.

## Reusable primitives likely to emerge

From PLAN-259 body: `checkoutTaskSnapshot`, `hydrateDatabase`, `startTaskServices`, `joinSharedNetwork`, `waitForHealth`, `runSmokeProbe`, `captureFailureState`, `resetTask`. These should be extracted when they recur across bounties, not speculatively.

## Non-goals — moved to the follow-up project

* `linear-agent` / `jules` / Render feedback-loop wiring → *LinearAgentBridge project*
* `nuclei` template integration → PLAN-258 v1
* multi-agent fleet over Linear subdomains → PLAN-258 v2
* Open-source + blog post for LinearAgentBridge → PLAN-257

## Related issues

* **PLAN-259** — anchor (BountyBench as calibration environment)
* **PLAN-328** — prior art (Argo/ttl.sh workflows)
* PLAN-258 — TDD mechanism (implemented in follow-up)
* PLAN-257 — LinearAgentBridge GTM (follow-up)
Creator: Robin Jordan

Name: Jules × Render autonomous fix loop (v0 plumbing proof)
Summary: v0 plumbing proof: Jules GH App fixes one dummy failing test via the existing Render preview + ssh tunnel + Dagger pipe. Zero custom webhook code. Unblocks Post 2 (Smallweb cron scheduling) and Post 3 (13×3 tidelanes).
Description:
'> v0 plumbing proof for the Jules autonomous fix loop. Held until gating is verified — read the three seed documents before creating any issues.

## What this project proves

One concrete outcome: a Jules-authored PR, opened in response to a Jules-labelled GitHub issue, takes a failing test from red to green through the existing `null-hype-dagger-pr-check` Render preview without a human touching the CI pipeline. The failing test can be anything — a wrong return value, a missing function body, a mismatched assertion. What matters is that **the loop closes on third-party infra we already pay for**.

## Why it's cheap

Every hop already exists:

* **Jules GitHub App** (Google-operated, default integration mode) — handles ingest, PR creation, and iteration on check-run failures natively. No custom webhook code.
* **Render preview** `null-hype-dagger-pr-check` (already defined in `render.yaml`) — builds on every PR, runs `dagger call -m ./infra module-check --src=infra` via ssh tunnel to a remote Docker host.
* **Dagger** — already set up, already wired to the ssh tunnel via `REMOTE_DOCKER_SSH_TARGET`.

Total novel infrastructure for v0: **zero**.

## Position in the three-post arc

* **v0 (this project)** — Plumbing proof. Any failing test. No BountyBench, no scheduling, no Linear hop.
* **Post 2** — Adds a custom scheduler via Smallweb cron + `.smallweb-root/linear-agent`. Agents on a schedule, safely. Project container already exists as `LinearAgentBridge — Autonomous BB Calibration Loop` (`085c6bd0-8263-4686-8dd1-4f5399212e5c`) and will be renamed when Post 2 starts.
* **Post 3** — Full 13×3 tidelanes per PLAN-69 and the *Architectural Thesis: Linear as Headless Agent Operating System* document. Pkl grammar as single-artifact docs/config/constraint/test/liveness. Langfuse inverse threshold monitoring. Consulting-grade SLA via coverage heatmap.

Each rests on the previous being unambiguously green first.

## Seed documents

Three documents are attached to this project and must be read by any delegate agent before issues are created:

1. **Charter & thesis** — scope, non-goals, gating, proof bar
2. **Architecture sketch** — the concrete wire, entry points in the current repo, required external setup
3. **Handoff brief for delegate agent** — order of operations, discipline rules, anti-scope-creep

The delegate should **propose** an issue seed set after reading all three and verifying the gating conditions, then wait for operator approval before creating issues.

## Related

* BountyBench Dagger Calibration Harness (`fd3aa958-8261-47f2-86bb-3f103158c426`) — parallel Post 1 calibration methodology project, in-flight in a separate worktree
* LinearAgentBridge — Autonomous BB Calibration Loop (`085c6bd0-8263-4686-8dd1-4f5399212e5c`) — Post 2 container (future)
* PLAN-69 — Tidelanes Pkl grammar, Post 3 substrate
* *Architectural Thesis: Linear as Headless Agent Operating System* document — Post 3 thesis source
Creator: Robin Jordan

Name: LinearAgentBridge — Autonomous BB Calibration Loop
Summary: Post 2 — held until v0 is green. Close the TDD loop so Jules autonomously fixes the bounty build via linear-agent, jules proxy, and Render. Supports PLAN-257 open-source + write-up.
Description:
'> **Held until the *BountyBench Dagger Calibration Harness* project has landed one green flag end-to-end.** This is Post 2 — the "I wired it into Linear and Jules fixed the build autonomously" story.

## What this project adds on top of v0

Starting from the working Dagger-modularized Lunary harness, close the TDD loop per PLAN-258:

1. **GitHub webhook ingress on the** `jules` Smallweb app — so PR events from Jules-authored PRs land on the corresponding Jules session by ID.
2. **Render deploy-status →** `jules` — webhook or periodic poll. Failing build logs get piped back into the session via `POST /api/sessions/:id/message`. This is the close of the TDD feedback loop from PLAN-258.
3. `jules` → `linear-agent` fan-in — so build status round-trips through `linear-agent` and is posted to Linear as `AgentActivityCreate` mutations. Preserves the invariant that Linear credentials live exclusively in `linear-agent`.
4. **Session state machine** — reconciliation across Linear issue ↔ Jules session ↔ GitHub PR ↔ Render deploy. Projection over the existing JSONL ledger.
5. **Delete or replace** `requestProxyOverSocket` — the hand-rolled TCP HTTP client in `.smallweb-root/linear-agent/main.ts` is a Smallweb platform workaround. Replace with first-class `fetch` as soon as cross-app routing is supported, or flatten the hop by giving `linear-agent` direct Jules credentials.
6. **Environment-diff guardrail executed as a Render preview check** — so agent-authored iterative fixes can never smuggle changes into the frozen target snapshot. Reuses the Dagger function from the v0 project.
7. **Open source** `LinearAgentBridge` + write Post 2 (PLAN-257).

## Why this is separate from Post 1

Different audience (agent-engineers + devops, not security/AI researchers), different claim (*automation*, not *measurement*). Gating on v0 also de-risks the demo: any bug in Post 2 is by process of elimination a wiring bug, not a calibration bug.

The viral framing of *"Jules happily writes exploits because it's just fixing a build"* is materially stronger once v0 already has the working Dagger pipeline — the loop is then the only moving part left to demonstrate.

## Architecture sketch

```
Linear webhook → linear-agent (HMAC verify, dispatch)
               → jules (POST /api/dispatch)
               → Google Jules API (AUTO_CREATE_PR)

GitHub webhook → jules (PR opened for session X)
Render webhook → jules (deploy status for PR Y)
               → Google Jules API (POST /sessions/X/message with failing log)
               → jules → linear-agent (HTTP callback)
               → Linear (AgentActivityCreate)
```

Invariant: `linear-agent` owns Linear credentials; `jules` owns Jules + GitHub + Render. They talk via HTTP. Neither reaches across.

## Related

* PLAN-258 — TDD mechanism (exploitation as failing build)
* PLAN-257 — GTM / open source story
* PLAN-259 — calibration thesis (implemented in the v0 project)
Creator: Robin Jordan
